sentences.text: From the Umschlagplatz.
That was on January 18.
When I-- when I-- when I came to, to the Umschlagplatz, I found my friend that we originally came to Warsaw together, and with his sister and brother-in-law.
And we were-- and one nephew.
And said, all right, we are here alone.
Let's stay together.
And we'll plan what to do.
And we decided that we're going to jump from the train.
And we made the plan that the first one to jump was my friend's sister.
She was-- I knew her from before the war.
We knew we were going-- we're going-- then we already knew that we go to Treblinka, to a, to a--
We didn't know exactly what Treblinka was.
We know-- we knew the name of it.
And, we know we were going to--
We're finished.
That's it.
So we had to take no chances, either this way or that way.
And we decided that the first one to jump was the-- my friend's sister.
And she would follow the train.
The next one to jump was the, the father and follow the train.
The next one to jump was the-- was I, and then the nephew, and then my friend.
While we were walking with each other, I could see that my friend did not jump.
When I jumped from the train, like, I would-- a feather would fall down on the floor, like I was swimming, such-- so easy that only a button from my jacket fell off.
It was a, a, a freight train.
It was a freight train, a cattle car.
They was all mixed.
No seats.
Everybody was sitting on-- if you find a seat, you could sit on the floor.
From the window.
The window-- I were to-- wanted to push up the other one.
On the roof-- on the top of the-- the roof of the train, there were soldiers sitting and shooting whoever was there.
They missed it.
And if-- 9 out of 10 used to hit the poles-- the electric poles or telephone poles along the railroad.
And if you jumped, you may fall into a pole and get killed without being shot.
When I got myself up, I turned myself over.
And I was holding and-- like this.
And I was-- lift myself like I'm swimming.
And I fell in just like nothing, like a feather would fall.
And nothing happened to me.
And I started to walk.
And I found the nephew-- my, my friend's nephew.
And we start to walk to see where to find my friend.
But we walked maybe a, a kilometer or two because he was supposed to be right after me.
And we decided-- that he made that he decided not to jump because he lost his wife.
He lost his child.
I was very depressed.
And I took my nephew, and going back to meet the father and the mother.
And both of them were bloody in such a form that I could not take a chance to go with them.
So I went into a farmer's house and knocked the door in the middle of the night to let us in to wash them up.
And we promised her to give her money.
So she let us do it.
I cleaned him up with something.
And we were ready.
It came already morning.
We had to go to the train and board going back to Warsaw because he had a place in Praga, which half of the Warsaw.
And I had a place in Warsaw.
So-- And at that time, I knew that when I went into the ghetto, my brother left also in another place.
I said, I'll go to my brother for the meantime and see what happens.
What kind of train did you take to go back on?
A passenger train.
I took-- I took-- when I took tickets.
I bought tickets.
And we went, because I was not afraid of myself because my looks were as an Aryan.
I always had money on me.
And going back about the money, when I came to pay for the woman, they said that they had their money hidden in belts.
We, we, we usually had double belts that you could stick in the money inside.
I had also a double belt, money in that.
But I had separately money in my pocket that says I have money outside.
I gave her a few thousand zlotys.
I don't remember exactly how much.
And she was satisfied.
[INAUDIBLE] this.
And I-- the train came up to-- up to Praga.
They, they off.
I also got off.
And I walked towards the door to my brother's place.
I wasn't in the ghetto yet.
Yes, that's right.
I was hiding out the ghetto.
I had at that time the identification Mieczyslaw.
The other name I forgot.
My brother also-- my brother also had identification.
But he looked like any other Jew.
He couldn't show himself.
Only, only when it's-- he could travel at night and with such a uniform that you couldn't see it anyhow.
Regular clothing.
Yeah, no-- nothing.
Yes.
At the time-- the first time I was in the ghetto from the end of December till January 18, we did not think of any uprising.
We would think how to make a living.
My cousins had their, their-- where they lived was on the-- at the gate.
Not at the gate, but at the wall-- but at a wall of the ghetto.
Let's say here, here is the ghetto.
Then there's a wall, then it's the outside.
And the entrance to the sewers was the reason we're going to have entrance to the sewer.
And just a few steps on the other side is the-- is a-- an exit to run away.
Yes, they were aware of it.
But, but we could listen from the other whether we can go or we cannot go.
And you take-- they'll take chances.
If you go out in this exit, you go further.
You go to another street.
But as long as you're on the outside, you have chances to run away-- to go out in the middle of the night, that it's or something.
That-- but to make a living, to eat, my friends used to do business with Poles.
Used to bring in-- the Poles, they used to work in the sewers, sanitation people.
They used to bring in through the sewers.
And from them, they got the idea how to run away.
They brought in hams, and meats, and breads, and all kinds of food.
All kinds of food that you wanted.
And they used to sell it to, to other people.
And so food was no problem living with my cousins to live.
And while I was there, I used to help them out, you know, to, to pack it, unpack it, to deliver it.
Something whenever it was possible, and watch out that we don't-- not being caught.
Oh, yes.
You could see the, the, the, the poorness on the streets, people laying on the streets dead, half dead.
People used to be one with one shoe, another with no shoes.
And a third one was already dead.
Some other one passed by and took off his shoes or his jacket.
It was terrible.
I used to see people from the Hevra Kadisha, the people that bury the-- bury the dead.
Used to walk around with a wagon, with a push cart, and put in like, like you take a sack of potatoes and throw them out.
There probably were people-- I know-- I know from my cousins that there were people who used to come in.
They used to give them food, because they had.
And they used to help.
And a lot of people didn't help because they were afraid tomorrow they would not have.
So everybody was more selfish.
I wouldn't say that all.
But there were a few, they-- I would say 65-35 or 70-30 more selfish.
About educational, I couldn't tell you, because I did not-- was at this time that I lived in Warsaw for a long time.
So I didn't know.
But, but nightclubs, there were nightclubs.
And I went myself into once or-- once or twice with my friends, with my cousins.
Yeah, dancing, singing, like, like before the war.
And there used to be songs, let's say, I'll sing it to you in Yiddish.
[SPEAKING IN YIDDISH] gold pieces, [YIDDISH] gold paper-- dollar paper.
[YIDDISH] Gold  dollars.
[YIDDISH] Soft ones.
Paper dollars.
[YIDDISH] That you have to go to the bath.
[SINGING IN YIDDISH]
We were-- we were--
Aware of it.
And we would make-- not to say we made nothing out of it.
We had no choice, at least, to--
Well, yes, there were Jewish police.
Yes.
And the Jewish police, of course, they-- many, many people looked bad at the Jewish police.
Of course, they were-- they had no-- nothing much in common with me.
I was a stranger.
And the rule was get off the street.
And if I didn't want to go, they had to push me.
And so a lot of people says, what do you mean?
I am older than you.
How dare you push me?
Things like that.
It might have been another friend of his or a relative.
He didn't treat that way.
They were blamed.
Many, many times the police should be blameless, because they had no choice.
They had no choice.
Why were you not allowed to be there?
In--on the street.
Because it was too many-- to be much on the street would make the Germans think that we planning something.
And they would come here, beat us up, or start shooting.
And we was dead.
To congregate in a large group was not feasible.
It was not right.
I couldn't tell you much because I was only at that time-- I was only a few-- about two weeks until January 18.
Later on, I came back.
It was a longer time.
But it was a different situation.
Yes, when I left for Treblinka-- when I jumped from the train, I came back, and I stayed with my brother for a few days.
And I've decided what to do.
The ghetto at that time until January 18th and till later were two ghettos-- a large ghetto and a small ghetto.
The small ghetto consisted, they said, mostly of professional, like, shoemakers, and tailors, and steel workers that could produce for the Germans.
To be able to go and to live in that, you had to have a place to-- of work.
So we decided-- my brother and I decided we'll go into the small ghetto.
We'll pay somebody money to make believe that we working for them.
But they couldn't take it.
We didn't-- first of all, they, they couldn't take any more employees.
And they did not have the place for us even to, to be there.
So we decided not to.
At that time, I with-- my brother and I decided that we should not be together.
In case anything happened, it should not happen with both of us together, and I hide another place, not the place that I was originally with my friend, because I couldn't go back there alone.
But not far from him, there was a man that wanted to take me in.
So I was there for about a week to 10 days.
And every day, I heard him say, oh, the bread today is more money.
And I don't know what it's going to be.
And I have seen a guy-- one of these days-- either give me the money or get out.
So I went back to my brother and told him that I have decided to go in-- and that a ghetto is still a ghetto and people go in and out.
And they still make a living with this and that.
And I did not know that my cousins-- the rest of them-- the one that was killed-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no, he was killed there the second time, not the first time.
Was killed-- that they were still there.
So when I came back to the ghetto the-- with my brother, we found the cousins there.
And they told us how they ran away.
And somewhere I told you before that my cousin was killed from them.
That was when I went to Majdanek.
That was in the May-- in the uprising.
I see.
In the uprising?
Yeah.
They lived there.
Yeah, they lived there in apartments.
There was no landlord to say, if you didn't me rent, you go out.
There were apartments there.
If there was one to take, because some of the apartments were taken.
There were less apartments--
He  was living in the ghetto.
You couldn't be outside.
I wanted to pay to get at-- to get a place that I live here, and I work here.
Not so much to live there as to work there.
Well, people had babies.
I don't know if they got married, because we did not go out much.
Everybody was-- where I lived was almost at the-- at the end of a street.
It was Milas 68.
It was almost at the-- at the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] the gate.
The gate to go in was a different place-- at the wall, at a wall.
And there wasn't much traffic there.
And everybody was trying to see, to get some ammunition, to get some guns to defend themselves in case anything happens.
A lot of where we were, we had a few guns.
But we didn't use maybe two or three times one of the cousins had a gun that they shot where they missed-- whether they hit or missed, I-- I don't know.
Because we didn't want them to know that we have guns.
If they hear that from this area, come shots, they'll come--
He didn't practice.
Maybe he was shooting back at a-- at a german, was himself alone.
So I don't remember the-- what circumstances.
Yes, there were plans.
But the uprising was not to say one command.
Each area, or each section of a few buildings had one or two connections.
And that they were work-- each one worked on himself.
Because you couldn't go out, and say, look, go defend this place.
Or go defend that place.
They need it.
Each area had their own fightings.
The, the, the people that lived in the area.
Because we realized at that time, we already knew that the, the story that we were supposedly being shipped east to labor camps.
And we should take the-- pack up everything, the most necessary things that you need, for it, not knowing that we, we go to Treblinka.
And it, it finished.
We already found out.
And we didn't care.
So we said, whatever it is, we don't go any further-- either you or I.
In my area, we, we had-- we lived in one side of the street with a building about four or five floors.
And the other side of the street was also a similar building.
And on the same street, were another few buildings that already had people.
So we were in touch with each other.
In case what happened, either we will take them in in case they would get caught.
Or they will take us in.
Or come together and they had the weapons.
And we had some weapons.
Altogether, we made about, about four, six buildings that we knew each other.
And that was supposed to be one area that we will fight.
But we could not fight living here and go fight 10 blocks away or, or to go through the streets.
You had no way because the Germans were on the street.
Each, each building had their own men that we were listening to.
Let's say, in my-- where I was, my cousin, the eldest cousin, he-- and this brother-- one different than the other one-- about a two-year difference.
Then--
Yes.
We did not belong to-- maybe the different organizations-- the other places that there were.
See, Warsaw was a place that was not like here.
You'd go into a building.
You'd go.
And you'd have a key to the door.
You take the elevator, and you go up.
A doorman, not a doorman.
He's taking care of the house-- he's cleaning the whole thing.
Superintendent.
He used to be 9, 10 o'clock at night or 11 o'clock.
He closed the door.
This was his living.
You had to ring the bell for him to open the door.
So this is how he made it.
So they used to pay to get a job like that.
They had to pay, because the tips they used to get to let in was a lot of money.
So you could go in into a gate.
And in the gate were different entrances to the buildings-- walk-ups sometimes.
And one of the houses had, like-- my aunt lived in-- the one that was in Israel that is in the picture, she lived on the sixth floor with an elevator.
But 9 out of 10 times, the elevator was broken.
So we used to walked up six flights three, four times a day.
So--
And in the-- in the center was like a yard.
We used to come out from the-- from the houses.
We used to congregate there and talk what to do with this and that, various things.
But these, these were the separate groups.
Together, with the other houses, we consisted of one group.
But if something would happen to us, to the building where I am, the other group supposed to come up and fight the Germans to let it-- to take away the things for us.
But who knows whether they are still around?
Maybe they, they, they were caught before.
Or maybe they're afraid they're-- maybe they don't have enough men or enough weapons to go out and help us out.
Everybody was hiding for himself and see how we can live by it.
Who planned when the-- when the fighting should begin?
We didn't plan it.
See, there was attacks.
The Germans came in and started to get the Jews out.
Not the selekt-- The selektsia was no selektsia anymore.
Selektsia was in the camps.
When you came into a labor camp, to a concentration camp, there was selektsia.
You go here.
And they go there.
This was aktsia.
This was action.
They came out to get out of the houses-- take you to the concentration camp, period.
If you don't want to, you'd be shot.
You cannot walk, you'd be shot.
You, you're a-- you're an old woman, you were a shot.
A young child-- what do we need him for?
There was no such question what to do with them.
Yes.
Yeah, I think it was April 20th or April 22nd-- something-- I get--
No, no.
That was the beginning, about Passover time.
Yes, I was there.
And from there I was taken to that Umschlagplatz where, where my friend was gassed, because he was gassed.
And that was the second time.
That was the second time that we went to the Umschlagplatz, to the railroad.
And at that time, they shot my cousin from my hand.
And I had no choice, because I was-- inhaled a lot of gas and the poop in the sewers.
Because they already knew that we were running away from the sewers.
They were throwing gas in the sewers.
And I went to the train.
Whatever will happen will happen.
I had no power.
I had no means to run away.
Yeah, that's right.
No, I did not leave.
It started on the 19th of April.
I left on May the 8th.
I was there-- oh, close to three weeks we were in the ghetto in the bunkers, and going from one place to another almost three weeks.
So-- then--
No, because when we left-- when they found us, threw in the bomb into the-- into the hole, we had to leave, because they threw in, and we had to leave.
Because it was already open.
They could see us.
So before they came in to look down who was there, we were already in the sewers, running into another place.
We came to another place.
It was a bakery.
And we hid between the flour bags.
And then they came there.
And there they took us out.
And we already had-- we were already down.
We were gassed and out of, of breath and so on.
And there they took us into the Umschlagplatz to send away.
Well, when I came to Majdanek, we had to go out.
And we were taken to the-- to take baths to take baths.
We had to have the belts put-- not in-- we had to undress with nothing on us and have the belt like this.
If it was a belt, a double belt, and they found a double belt with something in it, you got beaten up and maybe shot.
So a lot of people threw the belt away because the double belt had money.
Yeah.
We got off the train, the, the SS, the police-- the German police.
And there were the German police-- the German-- Ukrainians.
There were other ones, the Latvians, Lithuanians, the Poles and all the guards that were guarding us, that were taking us.
They brought us up to Majdanek.
And that's it.
And then Majdanek, as I mentioned, we went into the bath to give us a bath.
Before taking a bath-- and they took away the clothes, because we were with getting in camp clothes.
At Majdanek, we did not get striped clothes.
We got clothes from different people, because they did not want to because-- give us the same clothes, because we came in loused.
We had lice on us, because they were so clean, the Germans, they did not want to touch you.
And they, they-- we had to leave the clothes and get other ones.
And we had the [INAUDIBLE], and this is how we got into the camp.
There, there were-- in, in Majdanek, there were a few-- a few-- a few places, two or three that I remember, two or three places.
And when I came to Majdanek, a few days later, maybe-- not a few days, maybe two, three weeks later, I found out that my brother is there.
Because when he went in across the street, and we, we lived [INAUDIBLE], to find out what happened to the neighbors, the Germans were there and caught them all and sent them to Majdanek.
So he was already there.
And in Majdanek, they were sending away transports.
They were taking some of the people that were down, gassed, or little-- with-- unhealthy.
I mean, they, they could see that they're not going to be sent right away to the crematorium.
Majdanek?
Yes.
I wasn't seen as I am down.
But I was gassed, and I couldn't-- they sent us to work.
And I couldn't do it.
So they asked, who is sick to go to the hospital.
I had no choice.
If I'm not going to go to the hospital, I take a chance.
Maybe there's a hospital.
But if I'm going to remain there, I'm going to be shot on the-- on the spot there because I [INAUDIBLE].
When I came into the hospital, I found a man who was a son-of-law of people that we knew.
And he was brought from Warsaw in 1942.
I already came there in 1943.
And he was already-- and he gave me some pointers and-- but he wasn't there long.
Because I didn't-- when I came out of the-- out of the hospital alive, he wasn't there.
I couldn't see him again.
Maybe a week.
There were doctors.
No, they were Poles.
They were Poles.
Maybe they were Jewish, too.
But I remember that they gave me a drink to get phlegm down, because I was all plugged up.
He gave me a drink like a good beer, a, a liqueur-- like a cognac or something.
And when he gave me that, the whole thing got me so soft.
And I felt so good.
And I begged him for more.
He says, no.
But they had me on a diet and give me a hard boiled egg [LAUGHS] in the hospital.
Well, I came out of hospital alive.
And I was in Majdanek for almost three months.
Yes, I got a number in Majdanek, but not a tattooed number.
There in Majdanek, they gave us on a metal.
We wore it on the hand.
Yeah.
On a metal, it had the number.
Yes, we went out to work on the-- on the fields, dig ditches.
What these ditches were for I don't know.
But we dig-- we did dig ditches.
We were in, in the-- in the bunk-- in the bunks.
We used to have 8 to 10 and they were lined at three levels-- three levels.
And a matter of fact, I think in, in Majdanek, we had different beds.
[INAUDIBLE], I don't remember exactly.
We had different beds in Majdanek.
No.
We had bunks.
And in another place, we had different-- in Birkenau, we also had bunks-- 8, 10, or maybe more on, on one level, but three levels.
Yes, we were, because it came later-- it came later on after I, I came in the-- in May 8th or 10th to Majdanek, it came later-- other came transports.
Whether they-- whether I came with a transport, although I came at certain time, it was before the other ones were on Umschlagplatz.
Because I may came later, but there, there were people there before me.
They didn't take them.
They took us.
I don't know who came-- who went out of the-- of the Warsaw Ghetto before.
I knew what the outcome was, that there were another few week or two.
And there was a lot of butchering, a lot of killings.
